🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Speke Hall
*Country:* england
*Region:* northern england
*County:* Merseyside
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 16th century
*Coords:* 53.3368, -2.87422
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speke_Hall
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Speke%20Hall/@53.35,-2.87,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZOvGbk9J56eVTZL7Y2lZeQ8NcymEBnz0Pg44BAPWMe_j90d4IPoZDg-Tc0r3uvVMKE982xKYxPUxlrzHuIysnzWcWfqx9WO0f1zNtmREfN8Y-lUBtPm9Bd-NT8pNbAFfM_IW3m7gIPsXclzJg=s4800-w1200
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* unknown
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Speke Hall is a wood-framed wattle-and-daub Tudor manor house in Speke, Liverpool, England. It is one of the finest surviving examples of its kind. It is owned by the National Trust and is a Grade I listed building.

_Listing 1 of 80_

*Has Google Places:* unknown
*Enrich Notes:* add source - https://www.hevercastle.co.uk/

*Description:*
Hever Castle is located in the village of Hever, Kent, near Edenbridge, 30 miles (48 km) south-east of London, England. It began as a country house, built in the 13th century. From 1462 to 1539, it was the seat of the Boleyn family.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Cotehele House
*Country:* england
*Region:* south west
*County:* Cornwall
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 14th-16th century
*Coords:* 50.4959, -4.2257
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cotehele
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Cotehele%20House/@50.4917,-4.2267,15z
*Main Image:* https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Cotehele%2C_house_from_courtyard.jpg/330px-Cotehele%2C_house_from_courtyard.jpg
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* CDN
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Cotehele is a medieval house with Tudor additions, situated in the parish of Calstock in the east of Cornwall, England, and now belonging to the National Trust. It is a rambling granite and slate-stone manor house on the banks of the River Tamar that has been little changed over five centuries. It was built by the Edgecumbe family in 1485 after the original Manor House was pulled down. Sir Richard Edgecumbe came into the property after fighting for Henry Tudor in the Battle of Bosworth. He was gifted with money and the original Manor House and estate and then proceeded to build Cotehele.

_Listing 4 of 80_

listing.name: Cotehele House
listing.desc: Cotehele is a medieval house with Tudor additions, situated in the parish of Calstock in the east of Cornwall, England, and now belonging to the National Trust. It is a rambling granite and slate-stone manor house on the banks of the River Tamar that has been little changed over five centuries. It was built by the Edgecumbe family in 1485 after the original Manor House was pulled down. Sir Richard Edgecumbe came into the property after fighting for Henry Tudor in the Battle of Bosworth. He was gifted with money and the original Manor House and estate and then proceeded to build Cotehele.

imageAttribution.description: Cotehele National Trust property, house from courtyard.

items.text:
🔍 *ENRICH REVIEW*  \[✨ ENRICHED\]

*Name:* Kenilworth Castle
*Country:* england
*Region:* the midlands
*County:* Warwickshire
*Type:* castle
*Period:* 12th century
*Coords:* 52.3479693, -1.5923611
*Wikipedia:* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Kenilworth_Castle
*Maps:* https://www.google.com/maps/search/Kenilworth%20Castle/@52.3437,-1.5872,15z
*Main Image:* https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/place-photos/AJRVUZMTd7TcHGfxILV4xu_fiJWvK2soiATBTfRzZZIT2piJVPaHrkEjDM2KxrWDzrwVgC-y3tytpwqVtsHHITHv4y0Cha02YLIE25FRM1TjocpcKI1DxD2co7uTh5VN_ZOI3ClQYzV8fVOmpi40US4=s4800-w800
*Gallery:* 0 images
*Image Source:* google
*Has Wikipedia:* yes
*Has Google Places:* unknown

*Description:*
Kenilworth Castle is a castle in the town of Kenilworth in Warwickshire, England, managed by English Heritage; much of it is in ruins. The castle was founded after the Norman Conquest of 1066; with development through to the Tudor period. It has been described by the architectural historian Anthony Emery as "the finest surviving example of a semi-royal palace of the later Middle Ages, significant for its scale, form and quality of workmanship".

_Listing 5 of 80_
